problems.name: Drill Bit Slipping
problems.description: Drill bit slips or spins inside the chuck during operation instead of rotating with the drill.
visual_symptoms: Bit visibly rotating independently of chuck during drilling
Chuck jaws leave visible grip marks or flat spots on bit shank
Drilling progress stops while motor continues running
Scorch marks on bit shank from friction heat

probable_causes.cause: Chuck jaws worn or not fully tightened, providing insufficient clamping force
probable_causes.test: Tighten chuck firmly with chuck key or by hand on keyless chuck; attempt to twist bit by hand — any movement indicates insufficient clamping

probable_causes.cause: Round shank bit used in impact driver with hex-only chuck
probable_causes.test: Check whether bit shank is round or 1/4-inch hex; impact driver chucks require hex shanks

probable_causes.cause: Chuck jaws internally damaged or worn smooth and unable to grip
probable_causes.test: Inspect chuck jaw teeth under magnification; worn or rounded teeth indicate chuck replacement needed

steps.instruction: Loosen chuck fully, insert bit shank all the way, and tighten chuck firmly in all three chuck key positions
steps.verification: Bit cannot be pulled out by hand; no rotation independent of chuck detectable

steps.instruction: For keyless chuck, tighten by hand as hard as possible then give one quarter-turn with both hands gripping chuck
steps.verification: Chuck fully engaged; bit secure through full drilling cycle

steps.instruction: If chuck teeth are worn, replace the chuck with a matching size and thread chuck; use chuck key adapter to remove old chuck from spindle
steps.verification: New chuck installed securely; bit held without slippage at maximum torque setting

problems.name: Circular Saw Blade Wobble
problems.description: Circular saw blade wobbles laterally during cutting, causing inaccurate cuts, excessive kerf and potential kickback.
visual_symptoms: Blade visibly oscillating side-to-side at operating speed
Kerf width wider than blade thickness specification
Vibration felt through saw handle at full speed
Wavy or rough cut surface on workpiece

probable_causes.cause: Arbor nut insufficiently tightened allowing blade to run off-centre
probable_causes.test: Unplug saw and attempt to move blade laterally by hand — any play with nut not fully torqued confirms this

probable_causes.cause: Arbor flange or blade hub bent from previous blade jam or impact
probable_causes.test: Place a straight edge against blade at rest and rotate manually; deviation visible at any point indicates warp or bent flange

probable_causes.cause: Blade itself warped from overheating during previous use
probable_causes.test: Lay blade on known-flat surface — any rocking or gap indicates warped plate

steps.instruction: Unplug saw and tighten arbor nut with correct wrench using saw's shaft lock; torque to specification
steps.verification: No lateral blade movement by hand; nut fully seated against outer flange

steps.instruction: Remove blade and inspect arbor flanges for bending or damage; replace bent inner or outer flange
steps.verification: Both flanges flat and concentric; blade runs true after reinstallation

steps.instruction: Replace warped blade with a quality blade of correct diameter and arbor bore; never reuse a heat-warped blade
steps.verification: New blade lies flat on reference surface; saw runs without wobble at operating speed

problems.name: Angle Grinder Disc Crack
problems.description: Grinding or cutting disc develops a crack or shatters during use, posing a serious safety hazard.
visual_symptoms: Visible crack radiating from centre hole or edge of disc
Disc vibrates abnormally at operating speed
Disc fragments missing after use indicating prior partial fracture
Chipping or crumbling at disc edge during cutting

probable_causes.cause: Disc used beyond expiry date — abrasive discs have a maximum storage life of 3 years from manufacture
probable_causes.test: Check manufacture date stamp on disc blotter; any disc over 3 years old must be discarded regardless of appearance

probable_causes.cause: Side loading a cutting disc designed only for axial cutting force
probable_causes.test: Inspect disc type marking — Type 1 flat cutting discs are not rated for side grinding loads

probable_causes.cause: Disc dropped on hard floor before use causing internal stress fracture not visible externally
probable_causes.test: Ring test: suspend disc from arbor hole and tap with knuckle — a clear ring indicates intact disc; dull thud indicates internal crack

steps.instruction: STOP immediately and unplug grinder; do not use cracked disc under any circumstances
steps.verification: Grinder unplugged and cracked disc removed; disc disposed of immediately

steps.instruction: Install a new disc within its use-by date; perform ring test before mounting; tighten flange nut with supplied spanner wrench
steps.verification: New disc passes ring test; flange nut fully tightened; safety guard re-installed and adjusted

steps.instruction: Run new disc at full speed behind shield or pointed away from body for 30 seconds before first use
steps.verification: No vibration or noise anomaly during run-in; disc appears undamaged at rest

problems.name: Battery Not Charging
problems.description: Power tool battery pack does not charge or charger indicates fault, leaving tool unusable.
visual_symptoms: Charger LED shows red fault light instead of charging indicator
Charger shows no activity when battery is inserted
Battery charges but shows full immediately and depletes in seconds
Charger LEDs blink error pattern rather than solid charging indicator

probable_causes.cause: Battery cells deeply discharged below charger minimum threshold and charger refuses to start
probable_causes.test: Measure battery voltage across terminals — below 12V on an 18V pack indicates deep discharge requiring recovery charge

probable_causes.cause: Battery thermal protection triggered — cells too hot or too cold for safe charging
probable_causes.test: Allow battery to reach room temperature (15–40°C) and retry — charger should accept battery within 5 minutes

probable_causes.cause: Charger contact pins dirty or corroded preventing communication with battery management system
probable_causes.test: Inspect charger slot contacts and battery terminals for oxidation; clean with isopropyl alcohol and retry

steps.instruction: Store battery at room temperature for 2 hours if it has been in cold or hot environments, then retry charging
steps.verification: Charger indicator changes to charging mode within 60 seconds of insertion at room temperature

steps.instruction: Clean battery terminal contacts and charger slot contacts with isopropyl alcohol on a cotton swab
steps.verification: All contacts visually clean and shiny; charger accepts battery and charging light activates

steps.instruction: If battery is deeply discharged and charger refuses it, use a bench power supply to apply a gentle recovery charge at 0.1C rate until above minimum threshold voltage
steps.verification: Battery voltage above 14V on 18V pack; standard charger now accepts battery normally

problems.name: Overheating Motor
problems.description: Power tool motor overheats during use, triggering thermal cutout or causing permanent winding damage.
visual_symptoms: Tool stops suddenly and will not restart until cooled
Burning smell from motor housing vents
Motor housing too hot to hold immediately after stopping
Performance degrades gradually then stops completely

probable_causes.cause: Continuous duty cycle exceeded — tool used beyond rated on-time without rest periods
probable_causes.test: Review tool datasheet for duty cycle rating; compare with actual usage pattern — most tools require rest after 20–30 minutes

probable_causes.cause: Motor ventilation slots blocked by sawdust, debris or improper grip
probable_causes.test: Inspect all ventilation slots for debris blockage; compressed air reveals extent of obstruction

probable_causes.cause: Motor overloaded by forcing tool through material beyond its capacity
probable_causes.test: Current draw during use significantly above rated amperage indicates overload condition

steps.instruction: Stop tool immediately and allow to cool for at least 30 minutes with ventilation slots facing up in open air
steps.verification: Motor housing has returned to ambient temperature before resuming use

steps.instruction: Clear all ventilation slots with compressed air; inspect for debris obstruction around rotor area
steps.verification: All slots unobstructed; airflow felt through opposite slot when air is applied

steps.instruction: Adopt 20-minutes-on, 10-minutes-rest usage pattern and avoid stalling motor in material
steps.verification: Motor housing stays below 60°C measured by IR thermometer during standard duty-cycle use

problems.name: Carbon Brush Wear
problems.description: Carbon motor brushes wear down, causing power loss, sparking, intermittent operation or complete tool failure.
visual_symptoms: Excessive sparking visible at brush access points on motor housing
Tool speed fluctuates without trigger change
Tool fails to start intermittently
Grinding or clicking sound from motor at low speed

probable_causes.cause: Normal brush wear through expected service life — most brushes last 50–200 hours of use
probable_causes.test: Remove and measure brush length — below 6 mm (or less than 1/3 of original length) indicates replacement needed

probable_causes.cause: Accelerated wear from running tool at high load or with dirty commutator
probable_causes.test: Inspect commutator surface for grooves, blackening or uneven wear patterns

probable_causes.cause: Wrong brush grade installed during previous service causing rapid wear
probable_causes.test: Compare installed brush grade with manufacturer specification — wrong hardness or composition wears faster

steps.instruction: Unplug tool and locate brush caps on motor housing sides; unscrew and remove worn brush assemblies
steps.verification: Worn brushes removed; new brushes of correct grade and dimensions ready for installation

steps.instruction: Clean commutator surface with fine-grit sandpaper (400 grit) or a dedicated commutator stick to remove oxidation
steps.verification: Commutator surface copper-coloured and smooth with no deep grooves

steps.instruction: Install new brushes of the correct manufacturer-specified grade; run tool at no load for 5 minutes to bed in
steps.verification: Consistent tool speed; minimal sparking at brush access points; brush springs under correct tension

problems.name: Trigger Switch Failure
problems.description: Power tool trigger switch fails to activate, stays permanently on, or provides erratic speed control.
visual_symptoms: Trigger pressed fully but tool does not start
Tool runs continuously without trigger pressed
Tool speed jumps erratically with slight trigger pressure changes
Burning smell or discolouration at trigger mechanism housing

probable_causes.cause: Internal switch contacts burnt or welded from arcing under load
probable_causes.test: Measure resistance across switch terminals with trigger fully pressed — above 1 ohm indicates burnt contacts

probable_causes.cause: Dust and debris infiltration into switch mechanism causing mechanical obstruction
probable_causes.test: Compressed air into trigger slot — if debris emerges and function temporarily improves, contamination confirmed

probable_causes.cause: Variable resistor or Hall-effect element inside electronic trigger worn out
probable_causes.test: Measure voltage output of trigger circuit across full press range; erratic or non-linear output indicates worn sensor

steps.instruction: Unplug tool and disassemble handle to access trigger switch assembly
steps.verification: Trigger switch module accessible and disconnected from power circuit

steps.instruction: Replace trigger switch with exact OEM replacement part — matching current and voltage rating is critical for safe operation
steps.verification: New switch installed; full range of trigger travel produces smooth speed control from 0 to full

steps.instruction: Reassemble and test at no-load through full trigger range before returning to use
steps.verification: No unintended run-on; full speed achieved at full trigger; switch not warm after 5 minutes use

problems.name: Chuck Worn Out
problems.description: Drill chuck jaws are worn and no longer grip bits consistently, causing slippage or inability to hold bits securely.
visual_symptoms: Bits rotate inside chuck under load
Chuck jaws do not close evenly — one jaw stops before others
Drill bit wobbly or off-centre when spinning
Chuck key slots stripped, preventing proper tightening

probable_causes.cause: Jaw teeth worn smooth from extended use, no longer providing sufficient grip
probable_causes.test: Inspect jaw face teeth under magnification — smooth or rounded teeth without sharp profile confirms wear

probable_causes.cause: Chuck body cracked or jaw guide channels worn causing misalignment of jaws
probable_causes.test: Open chuck fully and visually inspect jaw travel — jaws should move in perfect parallel tracks

probable_causes.cause: Debris packed into jaw mechanism preventing full closure
probable_causes.test: Clean jaw mechanism with compressed air and brush; test if full grip is restored after cleaning

steps.instruction: Clean chuck thoroughly with compressed air and a small brush to remove all metal shavings and debris from jaw tracks
steps.verification: All three jaws move freely and symmetrically through full travel range

steps.instruction: If grip is still insufficient after cleaning, replace chuck: open jaws fully, use reverse-threaded retaining screw clockwise to remove, and thread on new chuck
steps.verification: New chuck seats fully on spindle; bits held without slippage at maximum torque

problems.name: Safety Guard Broken
problems.description: Protective guard on grinder, saw or drill press is cracked, missing or does not return to position, creating a safety hazard.
visual_symptoms: Guard cracked or has chunks missing from body
Guard does not spring back to closed position after tool contact
Guard bracket bent so guard rests at incorrect angle
Guard fixing screw stripped and guard rotates freely

probable_causes.cause: Physical impact from dropping tool or workpiece contact breaking the guard
probable_causes.test: Inspect guard exterior for impact marks or fracture point; identify if crack propagated from impact zone

probable_causes.cause: Return spring broken or dislodged inside guard pivot mechanism
probable_causes.test: Manually push guard open and release — guard should snap back; slow or no return indicates spring failure

probable_causes.cause: Guard bracket bent from kickback event, misaligning guard path
probable_causes.test: Sight along guard bracket alignment; any deviation from parallel with blade/disc confirms bent bracket

steps.instruction: STOP using tool immediately — never operate a power tool with missing or damaged safety guard
steps.verification: Tool unplugged and set aside; damaged guard noted for replacement

steps.instruction: Order OEM replacement guard assembly for the specific tool model; do not substitute with non-rated aftermarket parts
steps.verification: Replacement guard part number matches tool model number in manufacturer parts list

steps.instruction: Install replacement guard per manufacturer instructions; verify spring tension and auto-close function
steps.verification: Guard snaps to closed position when released; no cracking or binding throughout full travel

problems.name: Gearbox Noise
problems.description: Power tool gearbox produces abnormal grinding, whining or clicking noises during operation.
visual_symptoms: Grinding noise proportional to output shaft speed
Clicking sound at regular intervals during operation
Vibration in tool handle increasing with load
Metal shavings visible in gearbox vent holes

probable_causes.cause: Gearbox grease dried out or displaced, causing metal-on-metal gear contact
probable_causes.test: Open gearbox cover and inspect gear surfaces — dry or gunked grease with worn gear faces confirms lubrication failure

probable_causes.cause: Gear tooth broken or chipped from overload event
probable_causes.test: Remove gearbox cover and rotate output shaft manually; clicking at regular interval indicates broken tooth passing mesh point

probable_causes.cause: Gearbox bearing worn causing gear misalignment and uneven meshing
probable_causes.test: Check output shaft radial play by hand — more than 0.2 mm lateral movement indicates bearing wear

steps.instruction: Disassemble gearbox cover and clean all old grease from gear surfaces and bearing pockets with parts cleaner
steps.verification: All gear surfaces and bearing races clean and dry before relubrication

steps.instruction: Inspect all gear teeth for chips or breaks; replace any damaged gears with OEM replacements
steps.verification: All gear tooth profiles intact; no sharp edges or missing material on any tooth

steps.instruction: Apply manufacturer-specified gear grease generously to all gear meshes and bearings; reassemble gearbox
steps.verification: No grinding or clicking during no-load test after reassembly; noise level returned to normal

problems.name: RPM Fluctuation
problems.description: Tool speed varies erratically without trigger position change, affecting cut quality and safety.
visual_symptoms: Audible speed surges and drops during operation
Speed indicator gauge (if present) oscillating
Cut quality inconsistent — marks left on workpiece at speed dip points
Tool hesitates then surges on first trigger press

probable_causes.cause: Battery charge insufficient causing voltage sag under load, triggering speed controller protection
probable_causes.test: Test with fully charged battery; if fluctuation disappears, battery capacity or charge level was the cause

probable_causes.cause: Electronic speed controller module failing intermittently
probable_causes.test: Monitor current draw with clamp meter during fluctuation — sudden current spikes without load change indicate controller failure

probable_causes.cause: Worn carbon brushes causing intermittent motor circuit continuity
probable_causes.test: Inspect brush length and commutator surface; excessive sparking at brushes confirms brush contact instability

steps.instruction: Charge battery to 100% and retest RPM consistency under identical load conditions
steps.verification: Speed remains stable within 5% of set RPM under consistent load with full battery

steps.instruction: Replace carbon brushes and clean commutator if brushes are below minimum length
steps.verification: Sparking at brush slots reduced; speed stable immediately after brush bedding-in period

steps.instruction: If fluctuation persists with good battery and new brushes, replace the electronic speed controller module with OEM replacement
steps.verification: RPM stable across full load range; no surges during continuous operation test

problems.name: Power Cord Damage
problems.description: Mains power cord is cut, frayed, or has exposed conductors, creating an electrocution and fire hazard.
visual_symptoms: Visible cut, nick or abrasion through outer jacket exposing inner insulation
Exposed bare copper conductor visible in damaged area
Cord insulation melted, discoloured or hardened at one location
Intermittent power loss when cord is flexed at damage point

probable_causes.cause: Cord run over by workpiece or tool blade during operation
probable_causes.test: Inspect damage location relative to blade or wheel path; clean diagonal cuts indicate tool contact

probable_causes.cause: Repeated sharp bending at strain relief causing conductor fatigue and insulation cracking
probable_causes.test: Flex cord near tool entry point — crackling sound or intermittent power confirms conductor fatigue at strain relief

probable_causes.cause: Cord kinked and crushed under a heavy object causing permanent deformation
probable_causes.test: Inspect cord length for flattened or deformed sections with inner structure damage visible through jacket

steps.instruction: STOP using tool immediately; unplug from mains — never use a tool with a damaged power cord
steps.verification: Tool unplugged; no voltage present at tool confirmed with non-contact voltage tester

steps.instruction: Replace entire power cord with identical gauge, length and plug configuration using OEM or certified replacement
steps.verification: New cord wire gauge matches original specification; all connections secure with no exposed conductors

steps.instruction: Test insulation resistance of repaired cord with a megger between each conductor and earth at 500V — must exceed 1 MOhm
steps.verification: Insulation resistance above 1 MOhm on all conductor pairs; cord passes visual and electrical inspection

problems.name: Cooling Fan Failure
problems.description: Internal cooling fan stops spinning or is obstructed, causing rapid motor overheating even during light use.
visual_symptoms: No airflow felt from ventilation outlets during operation
Motor overheats faster than expected for workload
Rattling or scraping sound from inside motor housing
Fan blades visible through vents appear broken or jammed

probable_causes.cause: Fan blade cracked or broken from debris ingestion or age-related embrittlement
probable_causes.test: Shine torch through vents to inspect fan blades for cracks or missing sections while tool is unplugged

probable_causes.cause: Large debris (wood chip, fastener) jammed between fan blade and housing blocking rotation
probable_causes.test: Insert thin tool through vent to feel for obstruction; attempt to manually rotate fan while tool is unplugged

probable_causes.cause: Fan hub detached from armature shaft, spinning freely without driving airflow
probable_causes.test: Open motor housing and attempt to rotate fan on shaft — slippage with shaft stationary confirms detached hub

steps.instruction: Unplug tool and use compressed air to blast debris through ventilation slots; check if fan rotates freely after clearing
steps.verification: Fan spins freely when tapped by finger through vent slot; airflow restored from outlet vents on startup

steps.instruction: If fan is cracked or hub is detached, disassemble motor housing and replace fan assembly with OEM part
steps.verification: New fan seated firmly on armature shaft with no wobble; rotates concentrically without rubbing housing

steps.instruction: Reassemble and run tool at no-load; verify airflow and temperature remain normal after 10 minutes
steps.verification: Consistent airflow from vents throughout 10-minute run; motor housing below 50°C

problems.name: Torque Clutch Slip
problems.description: Drill-driver torque clutch slips at inappropriate torque levels, either over-tightening fasteners or disengaging too early.
visual_symptoms: Clutch ratchet sound occurring before screw is fully driven
Screws either stripped or not fully seated at same clutch setting
Clutch collar slips freely even at maximum numbered setting
No audible click at any setting indicating clutch not engaging

probable_causes.cause: Clutch spring tension lost or spring broken, causing inconsistent slip point
probable_causes.test: Set clutch to maximum and apply torque by hand — if clutch slips freely at maximum setting, spring is faulty

probable_causes.cause: Clutch balls and detents worn from extensive use, reducing friction torque
probable_causes.test: Disassemble clutch collar and inspect ball detents and ramp surfaces for wear or deformation

probable_causes.cause: Clutch collar number markings misaligned with actual clutch positions after reassembly
probable_causes.test: Compare clutch slip torque measured with a torque gauge versus expected torque for each setting

steps.instruction: Disassemble clutch collar by removing retaining ring and lifting off collar to access spring, balls and ramp plate
steps.verification: All clutch components accessible; spring, balls and ramp plate removed and inspected

steps.instruction: Replace worn clutch spring and ball detents with OEM components; clean all ramp surfaces with parts cleaner
steps.verification: New spring and balls installed; ramp surface smooth and undamaged

steps.instruction: Reassemble clutch collar and verify slip torque at setting 5 and maximum with torque gauge
steps.verification: Clutch slip torque within 10% of manufacturer specification at each tested setting

problems.name: Chuck Key Lost
problems.description: Chuck key required to tighten or release a keyed drill chuck is missing, preventing bit changes.
visual_symptoms: Chuck key absent from tool holder or dedicated storage clip
Bit cannot be tightened fully by hand on keyed chuck
Previous bit left in chuck because it could not be released
Chuck key socket teeth visible but no matching key available

probable_causes.cause: Chuck key misplaced or lost during use in workshop or on site
probable_causes.test: Search tool bag, bench and last work area for key; confirm key is absent from all likely locations

probable_causes.cause: Chuck key originally included fell off integrated clip on drill body from wear
probable_causes.test: Inspect drill body for key retention clip; check if clip is intact or broken off

steps.instruction: Identify the chuck key size needed by measuring the chuck key socket hole diameter and noting the chuck size marked on the tool
steps.verification: Chuck size confirmed (typically 3/8-inch or 1/2-inch for drills); correct key size identified

steps.instruction: Obtain a replacement chuck key of the correct size from a hardware store or online; mount it securely in a new retainer clip attached to the cord
steps.verification: Replacement key fits chuck socket securely; chuck tightens and loosens fully with new key

steps.instruction: Consider replacing keyed chuck with a keyless chuck of the same thread and arbor size for future convenience
steps.verification: Keyless chuck installed and tightens bit securely by hand without additional tools
